name: First Slide
description: Use this slide only as the opening slide of the presentation. It should appear once at the beginning, with a clear title and a short, clever subtitle that frames the topic.

name: Word Cloud
description: Use this slide sparingly to introduce a new section with a central concept and a cloud of related ideas, themes, or buzzwords. It is most useful when you want to show the broader landscape first and then unpack the individual parts in later slides.

name: Six Cards
description:
Use this slide to present up to six non-sequential items, such as tips, pillars, ideas, categories, mistakes to avoid, etc.
Use 3-5 cards typically. Only fill all 6 when the content genuinely warrants it (e.g., a long list of pro tips). Never pad to fill slots.
Each card must be a concrete, standalone point — not relative to other cards, not a buzzword.
Bad card titles: "Better triggering" (relative to what?), "Composable" (means nothing), "Scripts when needed" (a feature, not a point)
Good card titles: "Too many tools!", "Shellpocalypse Now", "MCP ≠ API wrapper", "Burns tokens fast"

name: New Section
description:
Use this slide as a section break before a new major topic. It should clearly signal a transition, with a short title and subtitle introducing what comes next. Subtitles are either \`""\` or a short quip/cultural reference. Never use the subtitle to summarize what the section covers.
- Bad: "Start with Anthropic's built-ins", "We'll explore the key concepts"
- Good: "Hold your horses", "Beware the thornéd rose", \`""\`

name: Quick Bridge
description: Use this slide as a short bridge within a section, especially when moving from an overview into a deeper dive. It is best for a simple transition statement or question that prepares the audience for the next concept, explanation, or breakdown.

name: Simple Message
description: Use this slide for a two-line idea where the second line supports, clarifies, contrasts with, or emphasizes the first. It works well for a brief idea plus example, a pair of reflective questions, a statement with a clarifying clause underneath, or a takeaway split into two parts for emphasis.

name: Image and detailed highlight
description: Use this slide to focus attention on one specific part of a screenshot, code image, or config image. It is especially useful in a sequence of slides that progressively highlight different areas of the same visual to explain a complex interface, snippet, or workflow step by step.

name: Exercise
description: Use this slide to introduce an exercise/project. The guidelines are optional for the participants. Each exercise slide has a link to a github repo already built in.

name: Custom Slide
description:
Placeholder for custom slides. Make sure to indicate the title as it fits in the sequence. The overview is a brief, few word explanation of the *purpose* of the slide, whereas the suggestion is your suggestion for how to get this objective across with a custom slide.
**Before reaching for \`custom\`, check if an existing template fits:**
Grid of labeled items → use \`6_cards\`
Two-line takeaway → use \`simple_message\`
Section break → use \`new_section_intro\`
Exercise/project → use \`exercise\`
An image/screenshot with explanation → use \`image_and_detailed_highlight\`

\`custom\` is for slides that genuinely need a unique visual: flow diagrams, side-by-side code comparisons, timelines, interactive exercises, input→output transformations.

The \`overview\` field is a **plain-language visual brief** — describe the layout a designer would build (shapes, arrangement, labels). Not content structure, not pedagogical purpose.
Bad: "Three layers: \`name\` and \`description\` first, full \`SKILL.md\` second..." (content structure)
Bad: "Four boxes. Each box says one concrete reason to use a skill." (use \`6_cards\` instead)
Bad: "Explain progressive disclosure" (pedagogical purpose)
Good: "Flow diagram: User → Claude → Tool → Result, with curved arrows and step labels"
Good: "Side-by-side screenshots of two code approaches with pink highlight boxes on the differences"
Good: "Horizontal timeline with 4 numbered nodes; short label above each, one-line description below"
